title: Causal Sequence Sidecar, Not Bidirectional/SMOTE Public Pipelines
summary: A recent pit-stop paper reports Bi-LSTM results with SMOTE and stratified validation, but those choices are risky for online next-lap prediction because bidirectional context and weak splits can inflate performance.
rationale: A recent pit-stop paper reports Bi-LSTM results with SMOTE and stratified validation, but those choices are risky for online next-lap prediction because bidirectional context and weak splits can inflate performance. Adjacent validation literature shows dependent-data leakage can materially overstate AUC. A safer bet is a small causal sequence model used only as an ensemble sidecar.
implementation_hint: Create fixed-length past-only sequences per driver-race and train a causal TCN/GRU or unidirectional LSTM under grouped chronological folds with purge/embargo between adjacent validation segments. Do not oversample with SMOTE across sequences; prefer class weights or focal loss. Blend its OOF probabilities with the existing tree model rather than replacing it.
expected_effect: May recover temporal patterns from recent lap evolution that static engineered features miss, while keeping leakage risk lower than public Bi-LSTM pipelines. Best use is as a diversity model in an ensemble.
risk: Higher engineering cost and easy to get validation wrong; sequence models may underperform strong tabular boosters if data volume per driver/race is effectively small.
